questions.question: A radioactive chemical has a half-life of $5\text{ years}$. If a container currently holds $80\text{ mg}$ of this chemical, how much will remain radioactive after $10\text{ years}$?
questions.explanation:
$10\text{ years}$ is equivalent to two half-lives ($10 / 5 = 2$). After one half-life: $80 rightarrow 40\text{ mg}$. After the second: $40 rightarrow 20\text{ mg}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The activity of a source drops from $1200\text{ Bq}$ to $150\text{ Bq}$ in $15\text{ hours}$. What is the half-life of the source?
questions.explanation:
Calculate the number of halvings: $1200 rightarrow 600 rightarrow 300 rightarrow 150$. That is 3 half-lives. $15\text{ hours} / 3 = 5\text{ hours}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student measures the count rate of an isotope over time. nnTime (h) | Count Rate (cpm)n--- | ---n0 | 480n2 | 240n4 | 120n6 | 60nnWhat is the half-life of this isotope?
questions.explanation:
The count rate halves from $480$ to $240$ in the first $2\text{ hours}$, and from $240$ to $120$ in the next $2\text{ hours}$. The interval is consistently $2\text{ hours}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Isotope A has a half-life of $2\text{ minutes}$. Isotope B has a half-life of $2\text{ hours}$. If you start with equal masses, which statement is correct after $1\text{ hour}$?
options: There will be much less of Isotope A remaining than Isotope B
There will be much less of Isotope B remaining than Isotope A
Both isotopes will have decayed by the same amount
Isotope A will have stopped being radioactive entirely
questions.explanation:
Isotope A will have gone through 30 half-lives in $1\text{ hour}$, while Isotope B has not even completed one. A will have decayed significantly more than B.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A radioactive sample has a half-life of $1\text{ day}$. After $3\text{ days}$, what $\frac{tion}{}$ of the original radioactive nuclei remains?
questions.explanation:
After 1 day: $1/2$. After 2 days: $1/4$. After 3 days: $1/8$. This follows the rule $(1/2)^n$ where $n$ is the number of half-lives.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
